Strongly-consistent KV store holding all cluster state. Uses Raft. Odd-member quorum (3 or 5). Source of truth.
controller manager
Bundle of reconcile loops as a single process: Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Endpoints, Job, etc.
scheduler
Picks which node runs each new Pod. Considers resource fit, taints, affinity, topology spread.
cloud-controller-manager
Bridges K8s to cloud APIs (load balancers, persistent disks, node lifecycle). Optional on bare metal.
kubelet
Node agent. Watches API for assigned Pods, runs them via the runtime, reports status. Also runs probes.
kube-proxy
Programs local iptables/IPVS/eBPF rules to implement Services on each node.
container runtime
containerd, CRI-O, or runc-based. Starts containers. Speaks the CRI to the kubelet.
Quorum
Majority of etcd members agreeing on a write. 3 members → quorum is 2; 5 members → quorum is 3. Lose quorum → read-only.
leader election
Mechanism using a Lease in etcd so only one of N controller-manager / scheduler replicas is active at a time.
kubeadm
CLI for bootstrapping a self-managed K8s cluster on your own VMs. Sets up the CP, joins worker nodes.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Control plane decides; nodes execute. Five control-plane components, three node components, one API door between them. Memorise the 6-hop Pod-creation flow.

✓ Lesson 13 complete

Control plane decides; nodes execute. Five CP components, three node components, one API door between them. Memorize the 6-hop Pod-creation flow — it explains 90% of K8s behavior.

Next — Lesson 14: The K8s API & YAML. apiVersion · kind · metadata · spec · status. How `kubectl explain` is the manual you didn't know you had. CRDs as first-class citizens.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 13 of 45 · grounded in kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/components · etcd.io · raft consensus
Lesson 14· The K8s API & YAML

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: City Hall — Permit Office.

📍 Permit Office · lesson 14 of 45Lesson 14 of 45, City Hall Permit Office.

Module 3 · Lesson 14 · how YAML becomes truth

The K8s API & YAML · Objects & CRDs

Every object is the same 4-section permit form: header (apiVersion+kind), identity (metadata), what-you-want (spec), and a blank for the inspector's stamp (status).

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

An engineer ran kubectl scale deployment api --replicas=10 to handle a slow service. It worked. Three days later, a CI deploy from the git-tracked YAML (which still says replicas: 3) ran. apply diffed, scaled DOWN to 3 mid-traffic-spike. 5xx surge. This lesson is about why imperative kubectl is dangerous, and why every K8s object has the same 4-section permit form.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Every K8s object is the same 4-block form (apiVersion + kind + metadata + spec). kubectl explain is the manual. Never use imperative kubectl in production. CRDs make custom types first-class.

Section 1 · Concept

Every K8s object has the same 4 blocks

Everything in Kubernetes is a typed object: a Pod, a Service, a Deployment, a ConfigMap, your custom Foo. Every object — built-in or custom — has the same four-block structure and lives in the same etcd-backed object store. Once you've internalized this, every kubectl apply feels familiar.

apiVersion: apps/v1          # 1. Which form template (group/version)
kind: Deployment             # 2. Which form
metadata:                    # 3. Identity (name, namespace, labels, annotations)
  name: my-app
  namespace: default
  labels: { app: my-app, env: prod }
spec:                        # 4. What you want (desired state)
  replicas: 3
  ...
# status:                    # 5. What's actually true (filled in by controllers, not you)

apiVersion + kind = "which API endpoint, which schema." metadata identifies the object. spec is what you declared. status is what controllers later wrote back — don't put it in your YAML, you don't own it.

API groups. The API is partitioned into groups so it can evolve independently. core (v1): Pods, Services, ConfigMaps. apps: Deployments, StatefulSets. batch: Jobs. networking.k8s.io: Ingress, NetworkPolicy. rbac.authorization.k8s.io: Roles, ClusterRoles. Each has its own version lifecycle (v1alpha1 → v1beta1 → v1).

kubectl explain is the manual. kubectl explain Pod.spec.containers shows the documented schema for any field. It reflects exactly what your cluster's API server accepts (including CRDs), not the docs site for some other version. Treat it as the source of truth.

Server-side validation. The API server validates every submission against its OpenAPI schema. Typos like replicaCount: 3 (should be replicas) are rejected before persistence. In CI, always kubectl apply --server-side --dry-run=server — that's the cluster's actual schema, not a client-side guess.

CRDs (Custom Resource Definitions) are the most powerful K8s feature. Define your own object type, apply the CRD, and the API server immediately serves it with the same RBAC, audit, validation, and storage as built-ins. Pair with a controller and you've extended K8s with a first-class new abstraction. cert-manager, Argo CD, Crossplane, Istio, Prometheus Operator — all CRDs + controllers.

Section 1.5 · The 4 blocks + CRDs

Each one, one tile

📑

1 · apiVersion + kind

which form, which version

Tells the API server which group/version to dispatch to and which schema to validate against. v1 alone = core group.

🏷️

2 · metadata

name · namespace · labels · annotations

Identity + selection. Labels are indexed and used for selection. Annotations are free-form metadata, not selectable.

✏️

3 · spec

desired state · you write

Your declared intent. Each kind has its own spec schema — see kubectl explain <kind>.spec for the fields.

🔖

4 · status

actual state · system writes

What's actually true. Read-only from your perspective. Controllers update it as they reconcile.

🧩

5 · CRDs

custom form types

Define your own object type. Same RBAC · audit · validation · storage as built-ins. Pair with a controller (operator).

All five compound: apiVersion + kind + metadata + spec + status + CRDs = the whole declarative API surface of K8s.

Section 1.7 · API groups

Where to find each kind

The API is partitioned so groups can version independently. Five you'll see all the time:

core / v1

Pod · Service · ConfigMap · Secret · Namespace · Node · ServiceAccount · PersistentVolumeClaim

apps / v1

Deployment · StatefulSet · DaemonSet · ReplicaSet

batch / v1

Job · CronJob

networking.k8s.io / v1

Ingress · NetworkPolicy · IngressClass

rbac.authorization.k8s.io / v1

Role · RoleBinding · ClusterRole · ClusterRoleBinding

Other groups you'll meet: autoscaling, policy, storage.k8s.io, coordination.k8s.io, admissionregistration.k8s.io, apiextensions.k8s.io (where CRDs themselves live). See kubectl api-resources for the full list on your cluster.

Section 1.8 · kubectl explain · the manual

Stop guessing field names

Your cluster ships with the manual built in. kubectl explain <kind>.<path> shows the documented schema of any field, with types and descriptions. Reflects your cluster exactly — including CRDs.

$ kubectl explain Pod.spec.containers KIND: Pod VERSION: v1 RESOURCE: containers <[]Object> DESCRIPTION: List of containers belonging to the Pod. Containers cannot currently be added or removed. There must be at least one container in a Pod. FIELDS: name <string> -required- Name of the container. Must be a DNS_LABEL. image <string> Container image name. ports <[]Object> List of ports to expose from the container. resources <Object> Compute Resources required by this container. securityContext <Object> Security options the container should run with. ... # --recursive prints the entire tree, all the way down.

Pro: kubectl explain certificate.spec --recursive works for CRDs the moment they're installed. No "is this in the docs yet?" guesswork.

Section 1.9 · Declarative vs imperative

Two ways to make changes — only one works in production

✓ Declarative

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

You write a YAML describing what should exist; the cluster diffs against current state and converges. GitOps-friendly: the YAML in git is the truth. Audit trail = git history.

✗ Imperative

kubectl create deployment … kubectl scale --replicas=10 … kubectl run …

Direct mutation. No diff. No audit. No git history of what changed. Fine for ad-hoc poking and learning. Disastrous in production: out-of-band changes silently divert from your YAML in git.

The strongest version of declarative is GitOps (Lesson 06): an Argo CD or Flux controller continuously syncs your git repo to the cluster, reverting any out-of-band kubectl changes automatically.

Section 1.10 · Labels vs annotations

Two metadata fields that look the same and aren't

labels

Indexed. Used for selection: Services find Pods by labels, Deployments manage Pods by labels, kubectl filters by labels. Keep them short, structured, and meaningful.

app: my-app · env: prod · tier: frontend

annotations

Free-form. Not indexed, not used for selection. Hold metadata humans or tools read: build SHA, deploy timestamp, on-call team, ingress controller hints, prometheus scrape config.

git-sha: a3f9c2 · deployed-by: alice · prometheus.io/scrape: "true"

Rule of thumb: if you'd ever write -l key=value to find it, it's a label. Otherwise it's an annotation.

⏸ Pause and check

Which of these is not a mandatory block in every Kubernetes YAML?

Answer: b. status is filled in by controllers, not by you. The four mandatory blocks are apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec.

Section 2 · Before & After

Five tools, five formats vs one form everywhere

The K8s API isn't just YAML; it's a uniform, validated object model that subsumes config formats from a dozen older tools.

Same 4 sections, different kind. Validated by one schema, stored in one etcd, audited by one log, secured by one RBAC. CRDs (last row) are first-class — same shape.
Before · 5 tools, 5 formats

Nginx config, Terraform HCL, systemd unit, Ansible YAML, crontab. Each validated by its own tool, stored separately, audited by different mechanisms. "What's running in prod?" requires inspecting 5 systems.

After · K8s objects

Same 4-block form for Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, CronJob, and your custom Certificate (CRD). Validated by one OpenAPI schema. Stored in one etcd. kubectl get all,certificates = the whole inventory.

What changed: a uniform object model. Every operational concern is now "just another typed object you read/write through one API."

Section 3 · Analogy

The permit office

Kubernetes is a permit office for your software. Every change requires a permit, and every permit is the same standard 4-section form.

You walk in with a filled form (your YAML). The form has four sections: at the top, the type-of-permit declaration (apiVersion: apps/v1, kind: Deployment — which form template, which version). Below that, the identity section (metadata: name, namespace, labels, annotations). Then the what-you-want section (spec — the only part you really write). The fourth section, status, is left blank — that gets stamped in later by the inspector (a controller) when they verify the work.

The clerk at the counter (the API server) looks up the form template, checks every field is filled correctly (server-side validation against the OpenAPI schema), and either accepts the form or points to the typo. If accepted, the form goes into the filing cabinet (etcd) and a watch event fires.

Different permit types are filed at different counters (API groups). core/v1 for Pods and Services, apps/v1 for Deployments, batch/v1 for Jobs. The handbook on the desk that lists every field of every form is kubectl explain.

If the office doesn't have a form for what you need, you can register your own form template (a CRD). Once registered, the office accepts your custom permits with the same validation and storage as the built-in ones. Pair it with your own inspector (controller / operator) and you've extended the office without forking the codebase.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The permit officeThe Kubernetes API server
The standard 4-section permit formEvery K8s object's structure
The type declaration at the topapiVersion + kind
The identity sectionmetadata
The "what-you-want" sectionspec
The inspector's stampstatus (filled in by controllers, not by you)
The handbook on the deskkubectl explain
The filing cabinetetcd
Bringing in a filled-out formkubectl apply -f
A custom permit template plus its own inspectorA CRD plus a controller (the operator pattern)

⚠️ The analogy stops here: a permit office has business hours. The Kubernetes API server is always open and serves thousands of requests per second.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

A permit office. Every form has the same four parts: what kind of form, who's asking, what you want, and a blank space for the inspector to stamp. The clerk checks your form for typos. If it's good, it goes in the filing cabinet. If you need a kind of form they don't have, you can invent one and they'll start accepting it.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

Every Kubernetes object is YAML with four blocks: apiVersion (which group/version), kind (which type), metadata (name, namespace, labels, annotations), and spec (what you want). A fifth block, status, is filled in by controllers. Resources are partitioned into API groups (core, apps, batch, networking.k8s.io...) each with versions (v1alpha1 / v1beta1 / v1). The API server validates against an OpenAPI schema — kubectl explain Pod.spec.containers shows that schema. Use kubectl apply -f (declarative, GitOps-friendly) instead of kubectl create / run (imperative, no audit). When K8s doesn't ship a type you need, write a CRD. The API server immediately serves it with the same RBAC, audit, and validation as built-ins. cert-manager, Argo CD, every operator — all CRDs.

⏸ Pause and check

You don't know if replicaCount or replicas is the right field name. What's the fastest way to find out?

Answer: b. Your cluster ships with the manual built in. Reflects the exact version you're running, including any CRDs.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where the API model shows up daily

kubectl explain saved hours of guessing the field name

A team integrating with cert-manager guessed spec.commonNames. Got "field not declared in schema." Tried spec.dnsName. Same error. Finally ran kubectl explain certificate.spec --recursive. Saw spec.dnsNames (plural). Five-second fix once they knew. Lesson: the cluster's own schema is authoritative; don't guess or copy from blog posts that may be from a different version.

Imperative kubectl scale disaster in production

An engineer ran kubectl scale deployment api --replicas=10 to handle a slow service. It worked. Three days later a CI deploy from the git-tracked YAML (which had replicas: 3) ran. apply diffed and scaled DOWN to 3 mid-traffic-spike. 5xx surge. Lesson: never use imperative commands on prod state — they don't show up in git.

cert-manager CRDs let teams declare TLS as K8s objects

Before cert-manager, getting a Let's Encrypt cert involved certbot, copying PEM files into a Secret, scheduling renewals. With cert-manager (a Certificate CRD + controller), you write 10 lines of YAML: "I want a cert for app.example.com, store in Secret app-tls." The controller talks to Let's Encrypt, completes ACME, fetches, writes the Secret, renews 30 days before expiry. Same kubectl apply workflow, same RBAC, same audit.

Server-side validation caught a typo before it shipped

A PR added replicaCount: 5 to a Deployment YAML. Local kubectl apply --dry-run=client did NOT catch it (client-side validation is shallow). CI used kubectl apply --server-side --dry-run=server. The cluster's schema rejected: unknown field "spec.replicaCount". Caught at PR review, never hit prod. Lesson: always --server-side --dry-run=server in CI.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch a YAML become a live object

Three modes. The lifecycle of a YAML submission, drilling down with kubectl explain, and registering a brand-new type via a CRD.

Watching: a YAML submission → validation → admission → etcd → controllers populate status.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: CRDs are second-class objects.
Truth: Once registered, they get the same RBAC, audit, validation, and storage as built-ins. cert-manager's Certificate and a Pod are equivalent in the API server's eyes.
Myth: kubectl run and kubectl create are the right way to deploy.
Truth: Those are imperative — direct mutations with no audit trail. Production should always use kubectl apply -f (and ideally GitOps on top).
Myth: Labels and annotations are interchangeable.
Truth: Labels are indexed and used for selection (-l app=foo). Annotations are free-form metadata, not selectable. If you'd ever filter by it, it's a label.
Four mandatory blocks of every K8s object?
tap to flip
apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec. A fifth block (status) is filled by controllers, not by you.
What does apiVersion tell the API server?
tap to flip
Which API group + version. Format: group/version (e.g., apps/v1) or just v1 for core. Each group has its own version lifecycle.
Name 4 API groups + a resource each.
tap to flip
core/v1 (Pod), apps/v1 (Deployment), batch/v1 (CronJob), networking.k8s.io/v1 (Ingress), rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 (Role).
What is kubectl explain?
tap to flip
The cluster's built-in schema browser. kubectl explain Pod.spec.containers drills into a field. --recursive shows the whole tree. Reflects your cluster including CRDs.
Declarative vs imperative kubectl?
tap to flip
Declarative: kubectl apply -f file.yaml — diffs against state, GitOps-friendly. Imperative: kubectl create/run/scale — direct mutation, no audit. Always declarative in production.
Labels vs annotations?
tap to flip
Both in metadata. Labels are indexed and used for selection (-l app=foo). Annotations are free-form metadata (build SHA, on-call). If you'd ever -l search for it, it's a label.
What is a CRD?
tap to flip
Custom Resource Definition. A YAML object that registers a new type with the API server. Same RBAC, audit, validation, storage as built-ins. Combine with a controller for the operator pattern.
Why --server-side validation in CI?
tap to flip
kubectl apply --server-side --dry-run=server validates against the actual cluster's OpenAPI schema. Catches typos like 'replicaCount' (real field is 'replicas') and CRD-specific fields client-side validation misses.

You're staring at this YAML and don't know if it's valid. What's wrong, and what's the fastest way to discover it?

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  replicaCount: 3
  template: ...
answerThe field is replicas, not replicaCount. Fastest discovery: kubectl explain deployment.spec — lists every valid top-level field of a Deployment spec with the type. You'll see replicas <integer> and immediately know what to use. --recursive for the full tree. To catch this in CI: kubectl apply --server-side --dry-run=server talks to the actual API server and validates against the OpenAPI schema. Returns error: unknown field "spec.replicaCount". Client-side dry-run won't catch it because client-side validation is shallow.

An engineer ran kubectl scale deployment api --replicas=10 to handle a traffic spike. Three days later a CI deploy from git-tracked YAML (which has replicas: 3) ran. What happened, and what's the right pattern going forward?

answerThe CI deploy ran kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml. apply diffed desired (replicas: 3) against actual (replicas: 10) and scaled DOWN to 3 mid-spike. 5xx surge. Root cause: imperative commands mutate state without updating any YAML in git, so "truth" diverges silently. Right pattern: (1) Always edit YAML, commit, kubectl apply from CI. (2) Better: use Argo CD or Flux to auto-sync git → cluster. With GitOps, git is the single source of truth and out-of-band kubectl changes get reverted (or flagged as drift) automatically.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

Your YAML has replicaCount: 3 (the right field is replicas). You run kubectl apply --dry-run=client. It passes. You commit. Click to see what happens at deploy. ▼

at deploy--dry-run=client only does shallow client-side validation — it doesn't know your cluster's actual schema. The deploy goes to prod. The API server rejects: unknown field "spec.replicaCount". CI fails. Incident. Fix: always use kubectl apply --server-side --dry-run=server in CI. That talks to the actual API server and catches typos like this — including CRD-specific fields client-side validation misses.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
apiVersion
Group/version string at the top of every YAML. Tells the API server which schema to dispatch to. v1 = core; apps/v1, batch/v1, etc. = grouped.
kind
The object type within the apiVersion. Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Certificate, etc.
metadata
Identity block: name, namespace, labels (indexed, used for selection), annotations (free-form metadata).
spec
Desired state — the only block you really write. Each kind has its own spec schema visible via kubectl explain.
status
Actual state, written by controllers. Read-only for you. Don't include it in your applied YAML.
API group
Partition of the API allowing independent versioning. Each group has its own URL prefix and lifecycle (alpha → beta → stable).
kubectl explain
Built-in schema browser. kubectl explain <kind>.<path> shows fields, types, and descriptions. Add --recursive for the tree.
OpenAPI schema
Machine-readable definition of every kind's structure. The API server validates submissions against it. kubectl explain reads it.
declarative
kubectl apply -f. You declare desired state; the cluster diffs and converges. GitOps-friendly. Default in production.
imperative
kubectl create / run / scale. Direct mutation. Fine for ad-hoc; banned in production.
CRD
Custom Resource Definition. Registers a new typed object with the API server. Validated, stored, RBAC'd, audited like built-ins.
operator
CRD + controller. Encodes domain knowledge (provision DB, rotate cert, manage cluster) as a first-class K8s primitive.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Every K8s object is the same 4-block form (apiVersion + kind + metadata + spec). kubectl explain is the manual. Never use imperative kubectl in production. CRDs make custom types first-class.

✓ Lesson 14 complete

One form, four sections, one office. Memorize apiVersion + kind + metadata + spec + status, learn kubectl explain, never use imperative kubectl in production, and know that CRDs let you add first-class types.

Next — Lesson 15: Pods Deep Dive. The fundamental unit. Multi-container Pods, init containers, sidecars, ephemeral containers, lifecycle states, and the patterns built on top.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 14 of 45 · grounded in kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects · openapi-v3
Lesson 15· Pods Deep Dive

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: Co-Living Quarter.

📍 Co-Living Quarter · lesson 15 of 45Lesson 15 of 45, Co-Living Quarter.

Module 3 · Lesson 15 · the atom of K8s

Pods Deep Dive · Init · Sidecars · Ephemeral · QoS

A Pod is a co-living unit. The containers inside are roommates sharing the kitchen and bathroom (network + IPC), with handymen who finished setup before move-in (init) and helpful housemates running alongside (sidecars).

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

Your team's web app needs Postgres ready before it starts. The Dockerfile has sleep 5 in the entrypoint as a "wait for DB" hack. Tonight, Postgres takes 7 seconds to be ready. Your app crashes on its first query. The Pod restarts. Crashes again. Restarts. Hits CrashLoopBackOff. This lesson is about the Pod patterns — init containers, sidecars, ephemeral debug — that turn this whole class of pain into one-line YAML.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: A Pod is the atomic unit of scheduling — one address, multiple containers sharing network and IPC. Init runs first, sidecars run alongside, ephemeral containers visit to debug.

Section 1 · Concept

The Pod is the atom of scheduling

A Pod is the atomic unit of scheduling in Kubernetes. Not a container — a Pod. The container is the unit of packaging; the Pod is what K8s actually places on a node and reasons about. 90% of Pods have exactly one container, but the rule that says "one or more containers, scheduled together, sharing some namespaces" enables the rest of K8s.

What's shared inside a Pod. Network namespace (same Pod IP, same port space — containers talk via localhost:port), IPC namespace (POSIX shared memory, semaphores), optionally PID namespace (shareProcessNamespace: true), and any Pod-level Volumes.

What's NOT shared by default. Each container has its own filesystem (mount) namespace and its own PID namespace. Each container is independently restartable.

The pause container. Under the hood, every Pod has an invisible pause container that's PID 1 in the Pod's network/IPC namespaces. Its only job: hold those namespaces open so individual app containers can come and go without losing the Pod's IP. You'll see it on a node with crictl ps; you don't write YAML for it.

Init containers run sequentially to completion BEFORE any main containers start. Sidecar containers run alongside the main one (log shipping, mesh proxy). Native sidecars (K8s 1.28+): declare in initContainers with restartPolicy: Always — starts before main, stays running, properly handled at shutdown. Ephemeral containers are added at runtime via kubectl debug, critical for debugging distroless containers.

Resources. requests = scheduler-reserved floor; limits = hard ceiling (CPU throttled, memory OOM-killed). The combination determines the Pod's QoS class (Guaranteed / Burstable / BestEffort), which controls eviction priority when the node is under pressure.

Section 1.5 · The 5 building blocks

Each one, one tile

📦

1 · Pod basics

atomic unit of scheduling

1+ containers scheduled together · shared network & IPC · separate filesystems · one Pod IP.

🔧

2 · Init containers

run-to-completion · sequential

Wait-for-DB · run migration · copy config. Pod stays Pending until every init exits 0.

🤝

3 · Sidecars

helpful housemate

Log shipper, mesh proxy, metrics scraper. Same Pod, same lifecycle, independently restartable.

🩺

4 · Ephemeral debug

kubectl debug · visiting

Add at runtime · doesn't restart Pod · debug distroless containers · ephemeral, leaves no trace.

⚓

5 · pause + namespaces

the invisible glue

Invisible PID 1 holding the Pod's net/IPC namespaces open. App containers come & go; the IP stays.

All five compound: pause + init + main + sidecar + ephemeral = the full container vocabulary inside one Pod.

Section 1.7 · Multi-container patterns

Three flavors of co-tenant container

Brendan Burns's classic taxonomy. Most production multi-container Pods are sidecars; ambassador and adapter show up less often but explain a lot of common architecture.

Sidecar

Augments the main container — log shipper, mesh proxy, metrics scraper. Most common pattern.

Ambassador

Proxies an external service for the main container — DB connection pool, OAuth proxy, retry logic.

Adapter

Transforms the main's output — translates app metrics into Prometheus format, normalizes log shape.

Section 1.8 · requests vs limits

The floor and the ceiling

request is what the scheduler reserves for you — the floor. limit is the hard ceiling — CPU above limit is throttled, memory above limit is OOM-killed.

CPU above limit is throttled (slow); memory above limit is killed (OOM). Set requests close to your steady-state usage so the scheduler packs efficiently; set limits with enough headroom for legitimate spikes.

Section 1.9 · QoS classes

Three eviction-priority tiers

QoS class is computed automatically from your requests/limits and decides who gets evicted first when the node runs short on memory.

Last evicted

Guaranteed

Every container has CPU and memory request == limit. Highest priority during node pressure. Use for: latency-critical, predictable workloads.

Mid eviction

Burstable

At least one container has a request, and request < limit (or limit unset). Most production Pods. Can spike above request when capacity is free.

First evicted

BestEffort

No requests or limits anywhere. Scheduler can't reserve capacity. First to be evicted under node pressure. Don't use for production workloads.

[ deep dive — skip if new ]Important: QoS doesn't grant extra memory. Even Guaranteed Pods get OOM-killed when they exceed their own limit. Class only affects eviction order when the node runs short. The eviction algorithm walks BestEffort first, then Burstable, then Guaranteed; ties break by oldest creation time.

Section 1.10 · restartPolicy

Three policies, three workload types

Always

Default. Restart any exited container regardless of exit code. Used by Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets — long-running services.

OnFailure

Restart only on non-zero exit. Used by Jobs that should retry on error but stop on success.

Never

Don't restart, ever. Used by Jobs you want to fail loudly so a human investigates. Pod stays in Failed phase.

restartPolicy is Pod-level — applies to all containers. Note: it controls kubelet's local restarts. Pod-level controllers (Deployments) have their own logic on top — they create new Pods if a Pod dies entirely.

⏸ Pause and check

What's the atomic unit of scheduling in Kubernetes?

Answer: b. The container is the packaging unit. The Pod is what Kubernetes places on a node. Most Pods have one container — but the rule "one or more containers, scheduled together" enables sidecars, init containers, and ephemeral debug.

Section 2 · Before & After

Multi-process container vs Pod with sidecar

The classic mistake first-time K8s users make: cramming app + log shipper into one Docker image with a shell wrapper.

Same workload (app + log shipper) — left bundles them in one image with a shell, right separates them as containers in one Pod. Independent images, independent restarts, proper PID 1 in each.
Before · one image, shell wrapper

Single Docker image with ./app & ./fluent-bit in a shell entrypoint. bash is PID 1, swallows SIGTERM, neither process is independently restartable, can't version them separately, scaling means scaling both.

After · Pod, two containers

Two separate images in one Pod. Main is just the app. Sidecar is just the log shipper. Each has its own image, version, lifecycle, resource requests, and restart count. Pod's pause container holds the IP open.

What didn't change: the app and the log shipper still cooperate. What changed: they're cleanly separated, independently versioned, and properly lifecycle-managed.

Section 3 · Analogy

The co-living unit

A Pod is a co-living unit. The containers are roommates. They share the kitchen (network namespace — they can chat from across the room via localhost) and the bathroom (IPC), but each has their own bedroom (separate filesystem, often separate PID namespace). The co-living unit has one doorbell (the Pod IP) that any roommate can answer.

Before anyone moves in, the handyman (init container) shows up, finishes the wifi setup, hangs the curtains, and leaves. Only then do the roommates move in.

The main roommate does the actual work. The sidecar housemate does laundry quietly in the background, or answers the door (ambassador), or translates when guests speak a different language (adapter).

When something breaks and the main roommate doesn't have a screwdriver (distroless image, no shell), a plumber can drop in temporarily (ephemeral debug container). They don't move in — they visit, fix, and leave. The co-living unit doesn't get evicted to bring the plumber in.

The building's superintendent (the kubelet) decides whether to give your co-living unit guaranteed rent control (QoS Guaranteed, last to evict), flexible rent (Burstable), or first to be asked to leave (BestEffort) based on how much electricity and water you reserved.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The co-living unit (one address, shared kitchen)A Pod (the atomic unit of scheduling)
The roommatesContainers inside the Pod
The shared kitchenThe network namespace (containers talk via localhost)
The shared bathroomThe IPC namespace
Each roommate's bedroomFilesystem & PID namespaces (per container)
The doorbell at the frontThe Pod IP
The handyman who came firstAn init container
A helpful housemate alongsideA sidecar container
A visiting plumberAn ephemeral debug container
The superintendent + rent-control classThe kubelet + QoS class (Guaranteed / Burstable / BestEffort)

⚠️ The analogy stops here: real co-living units have one front door but many bedrooms. Pods can share a PID namespace too (shareProcessNamespace: true), and any container can hold the Pod's IP — there's no "main bedroom" that's special.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

A Pod is a co-living unit. The containers inside are roommates who share the kitchen and bathroom. A handyman comes first to fix things up; then the roommates move in. One roommate works; the others help. If something breaks and you don't have tools, a plumber visits just to fix it. The building manager decides who has to leave first if the building gets crowded.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

A Pod is the atomic unit of scheduling in K8s — not a container. Containers in a Pod share network and IPC namespaces (they can talk via localhost) but have separate filesystems and PIDs by default. Init containers run sequentially to completion before main containers start. Sidecars support the main container; since K8s 1.28, native sidecars are init containers with restartPolicy: Always. Ephemeral containers are added at runtime via kubectl debug — critical for distroless containers. Each container has resource requests (scheduler-reserved floor) and limits (kill ceiling); the combination determines the Pod's QoS class. The pause container holds the Pod's network namespace open so app containers can come and go without losing the IP.

⏸ Pause and check

Your distroless container has no shell. It just started returning 500s. How do you debug it?

Answer: b. kubectl debug adds an ephemeral container to the running Pod. Sibling to the broken container, sharing its PID namespace. You get a shell without modifying the production image.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where Pod patterns pay off

Init container blocked startup until the database was reachable

Web app needed PostgreSQL ready before starting. Engineers had sleeps in the entrypoint, sometimes hit races. Fix: initContainers: [{name: wait-db, image: busybox, command: ["sh","-c","until nc -z db 5432; do sleep 1; done"]}]. Pod stays Pending until the init exits 0 — guaranteed db-ready. No more flaky cold starts.

Log-shipping sidecar replaced 12 different agents on each VM

Pre-K8s, every team installed their own log agent on each VM (different versions, different configs). Audit nightmare. After K8s: one standardized fluent-bit sidecar in every Pod template (or DaemonSet — Lesson 16). Logs flow to one collector. Independent images, independent versioning, one mechanism.

kubectl debug saved an outage when the main container had no shell

Production app started returning 500s. Image was gcr.io/distroless/python3 — no /bin/sh to exec into. kubectl exec ... bash failed. Engineer ran kubectl debug -it broken-Pod --image=busybox --target=app -- sh. Got a shell in a sibling container sharing the broken container's PID namespace. Found the bad file descriptor in seconds. Distroless safety + ephemeral debug = best of both.

QoS confusion: Guaranteed Pods kept getting OOM-killed

Team set requests.memory: 1Gi, limits.memory: 1Gi (Guaranteed). App spiked to 1.2Gi during bursts. OOM-kills every burst. They thought Guaranteed meant "always gets memory." It doesn't — exceeding limit = kill, regardless of class. Fix: raise limit (becomes Burstable) or right-size the app so peak fits under limit. QoS only affects eviction priority when the node is under pressure.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch the Pod come up · ship logs · debug at runtime

Three modes. Pod startup with init containers, a sidecar shipping logs, and adding an ephemeral debug container to a running Pod.

Watching: pause → init-1 → init-2 → main + sidecar.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: "QoS class Guaranteed" means the Pod always gets the memory it asks for.
Truth: Guaranteed only affects eviction priority under node pressure. Within the Pod, the limit is still a hard cap — exceeding it = OOM, regardless of class.
Myth: Sidecars are just "two containers in a Pod."
Truth: Native sidecars (K8s 1.28+) are init containers with restartPolicy: Always. Start before main, stay running, properly handled at shutdown. Old-style "just two containers" had lifecycle gotchas.
Myth: The pause container is something you write.
Truth: It's invisible infrastructure. Every Pod has one; you never see it in YAML. It holds the Pod's network/IPC namespaces open so app containers can come and go without losing the IP.
What is a Pod?
tap to flip
The atomic unit of scheduling in K8s. One or more containers placed together on a node, sharing network and IPC namespaces. Each container has its own filesystem and PID namespace by default.
What is the pause container?
tap to flip
An invisible infrastructure container in every Pod. Holds the Pod's network/IPC namespaces open so app containers can come and go without losing the Pod's IP.
What is an init container?
tap to flip
Container that runs to completion BEFORE any main container starts. Sequential. Uses: wait for a dependency, copy config, run a migration. Pod stays Pending until inits exit 0.
What is a sidecar?
tap to flip
Second container in a Pod that supports the main one (log shipping, mesh proxy). K8s 1.28+ native sidecars: initContainer with restartPolicy: Always — starts before main, stays running, properly handled at shutdown.
Multi-container patterns?
tap to flip
Sidecar = augments main (log shipper). Ambassador = proxies external service for main (DB pool). Adapter = transforms main's output (JSON → Prometheus).
What is an ephemeral container?
tap to flip
A container added to a running Pod via kubectl debug. Doesn't restart the Pod. Critical for debugging distroless containers (no shell). Can share PID namespace with target.
requests vs limits?
tap to flip
request = scheduler-reserved floor (won't pack node past 100% of requests). limit = hard ceiling (CPU throttled, memory OOM-killed above limit).
Three QoS classes?
tap to flip
Guaranteed (request==limit, last evicted), Burstable (request

Your web app needs PostgreSQL ready before it starts, otherwise it crashes on its first query. Current setup: single container with sleep loops in the entrypoint, sometimes races and crashes anyway. K8s-native fix?

answerUse an init container. Add to the Pod spec: initContainers: [{name: wait-db, image: busybox, command: ["sh","-c","until nc -z db.svc 5432; do sleep 1; done"]}]. The kubelet runs init containers sequentially before any main containers start. The Pod stays in Pending phase until every init exits 0. This guarantees the DB is reachable before your app's first byte runs — no sleep races, no entrypoint hacks. If you have multiple ordered prerequisites (wait-db, run-migration, seed-data), each is its own init container in order. Bonus: the app container's image stays clean — no netcat, no init logic.

Your production app uses a distroless base (gcr.io/distroless/python3) for security. It just started returning 500s. kubectl exec -it ... bash fails with "executable file not found in $PATH". What do you do?

answerkubectl debug. Run: kubectl debug -it broken-Pod --image=busybox --target=app -- sh. This adds an ephemeral container to the running Pod. The ephemeral container is a sibling to your main "app" container, sharing the Pod's network namespace AND (because of --target) the main container's PID namespace. You get a busybox shell that can ps, ls /proc/<main-pid>/, and tcpdump as if you were inside the broken container. The Pod doesn't restart. Once you exit, the ephemeral container is gone but the rest of the Pod is unchanged. This is the right answer for distroless: keep the production attack surface tiny and use ephemeral debug containers when you need to investigate.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

Your team sets requests.memory: 1Gi and limits.memory: 1Gi (QoS class: Guaranteed). The app spikes to 1.2Gi during bursts. Click to see what happens. ▼

every burstOOMKilled. Every burst. The team thought "Guaranteed" meant "always gets the memory" — it doesn't. It means "request == limit, last to be evicted under node pressure." Within the Pod, the limit is a hard cap. Fix: raise the limit (becomes Burstable but survives spikes), or right-size the app. QoS is about node-level eviction priority, not Pod-level memory grants.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
Pod
Atomic unit of scheduling. 1+ containers, shared network & IPC, separate filesystems by default. One Pod IP.
pause container
Invisible infra container in every Pod. Holds the Pod's net/IPC namespaces open so app containers can come and go without losing the IP.
init container
Run-to-completion container that executes BEFORE any main containers start. Sequential. Pod stays Pending until all inits exit 0.
sidecar
Second container in a Pod supporting the main one (log shipper, mesh proxy). 1.28+ native sidecars: initContainer with restartPolicy: Always.
ambassador
Pattern: a container that proxies an external service for the main container (DB connection pool, OAuth proxy).
adapter
Pattern: a container that transforms the main's output (e.g., translates app metrics into Prometheus format).
ephemeral container
Container added to a running Pod via kubectl debug. Doesn't restart the Pod. Used to debug distroless containers.
requests
Resources the scheduler reserves for the container. The "floor." Total requests across Pods on a node ≤ node's allocatable.
limits
Hard ceiling. CPU above limit is throttled; memory above limit is OOM-killed.
QoS class
Computed from requests/limits. Guaranteed (req==lim), Burstable (req<lim), BestEffort (none). Affects eviction order under node pressure.
restartPolicy
Pod-level: Always (default — Deployments), OnFailure (Jobs that retry), Never (Jobs that fail loudly).
shareProcessNamespace
Pod field. When true, all containers in the Pod share a PID namespace and can see each other's processes.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: A Pod is the atomic unit of scheduling — one address, multiple containers sharing network and IPC. Init runs first, sidecars run alongside, ephemeral containers visit to debug.

✓ Lesson 15 complete

One co-living unit, multiple roommates, shared kitchen. Init does setup; sidecars help; ephemeral visits to debug; pause holds it all together. Tune requests/limits to size the co-living unit.

Next — Lesson 16: Workload Controllers. Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs — the controllers that build Pods on top of Pods.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 15 of 45 · grounded in kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/Pods · sidecar-containers · Pod-qos
Lesson 16· Workload Controllers · Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: K-Town Dispatch Office.

📍 Dispatch Office · lesson 16 of 45Lesson 16 of 45, Dispatch Office.

Module 4 · Lesson 16 · how Kubernetes runs your apps

Workload Controllers · Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob

Pods come and go; workload controllers keep your apps running at the right shape. Five flavours, one pattern: declare what you want, the controller reconciles. Forever.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

You manually create five Pods for your web app and walk away. By 3 AM, traffic doubles. The Pods can't keep up. One crashes from memory pressure. Now you have four. Nobody's awake to notice. By 8 AM, two more have crashed. You're at two Pods serving rush-hour traffic, customers are seeing timeouts, and the on-call engineer's first task of the day is figuring out what just happened. This lesson is about the abstraction that makes that whole class of pain go away — and the five flavours of it for the five shapes of work.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Pods come and go. Workload controllers keep your apps running at the right shape — five flavours: Deployment for stateless apps, StatefulSet for apps with identity, DaemonSet for one-per-node, Job for run-once, CronJob for run-on-schedule.

Section 1 · Concept

What a workload controller is

A Pod is the unit of work — one or more containers scheduled together. But a Pod by itself is fragile: if it crashes, nothing brings it back. If you want three copies, you have to create three Pods. If demand doubles, you create more by hand. If a node dies, every Pod on that node disappears.

A workload controller is the loop that solves this. You declare what you want — "five copies of my-app:v2, healthy" — and the controller compares that against what's running, and creates or deletes Pods to close the gap. Forever. If a Pod crashes, the controller notices and creates a replacement. If a node dies, the controller reschedules the missing Pods elsewhere. If you change the desired count, the controller adjusts.

Kubernetes ships with five built-in workload controllers, one for each common shape of work:

Section 1.5 · The five flavours

One controller per shape of work

Each controller has the same loop pattern (declare, observe, reconcile) but a different idea of what the right Pod shape looks like. Pick the one that matches the work.

🔁

Deployment

rotating shifts

Manages a set of identical, interchangeable Pods. Rolling updates create new Pods one batch at a time and retire old ones once the new are ready. The default for stateless web apps, APIs, workers.

When: any app where Pods don't care about their own identity.

🪑

StatefulSet

assigned seats

Each Pod has a stable name (db-0, db-1, db-2) that survives restarts, plus a stable persistent volume per replica. Startup and shutdown are ordered. The right choice for databases, queues, anything that needs identity.

When: Postgres, Cassandra, MongoDB, Kafka, Elasticsearch, ZooKeeper.

👀

DaemonSet

one-per-node watchman

Runs exactly one Pod on every node (or every node matching a selector). Add a node, the Pod auto-deploys; remove a node, the Pod disappears. The pattern for log collectors, monitoring agents, network plugins.

When: Fluent Bit, Prometheus node-exporter, Calico CNI, kube-proxy.

📋

Job

one-time work order

Runs one or more Pods to completion. Once a Pod exits cleanly, the Job is done. If a Pod fails, the Job restarts it (up to a backoff limit). For batch processing, migrations, image renders, reports.

When: database migrations, video transcodes, ML training runs.

⏰

CronJob

scheduled round

A Job on a cron schedule. 0 2 * * * runs the Job at 2 AM every day. The CronJob controller creates a fresh Job at each tick; the Job creates the Pod; the Pod runs to completion.

When: nightly backups, hourly cache warms, monthly reports.

Section 1.7 · Deployment in detail

The most common controller — Deployment + ReplicaSet

A Deployment is a layer above the simpler ReplicaSet. A ReplicaSet keeps N identical Pods alive — that's the simplest reconciliation loop. A Deployment manages ReplicaSets so that you can update the Pod template (e.g. change the image tag) and the cluster transitions smoothly from the old version to the new one.

The rolling update is the key trick. When you update a Deployment's image:

  1. The Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet (with the new image) at zero replicas.
  2. It scales the new ReplicaSet up by maxSurge (default 25%) and waits for the new Pods to report ready.
  3. Once the new Pods are ready, it scales the old ReplicaSet down by maxUnavailable (default 25%).
  4. Repeat until the new ReplicaSet has all replicas and the old one has zero.

If a new Pod fails its readiness probe, the rollout pauses — old Pods stay up. kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app reverts to the previous ReplicaSet (which still exists, just at zero replicas). Rollback is one command.

[ deep dive — skip if new ]You'll rarely create a ReplicaSet directly — Deployments hide them. The ReplicaSet hash appears in Pod names (my-app-7d4b9c-xkz2p) so you can identify which version a Pod belongs to. kubectl get rs shows the active and historical ReplicaSets the Deployment has produced.

Section 1.9 · StatefulSet in detail

When Pods need a name

A StatefulSet is a workload controller that gives each Pod a stable identity that survives restarts. It's the right choice for any workload where Pods know about each other by name — replicated databases, message queues, distributed file systems.

Three guarantees a Deployment doesn't make:

Deployment (stateless)

  • Pods get random names (app-7d4b9c-xkz2p)
  • Restart → fresh name, fresh IP, fresh PVC
  • Startup order: any order, in parallel
  • Rolling update: replace any Pod at any time
  • Storage: ephemeral or shared

StatefulSet (stateful)

  • Pods get ordinal names (db-0, db-1, db-2)
  • Restart → same name, same PVC re-attached
  • Startup: ordered (db-0 before db-1 before db-2)
  • Shutdown: reverse-ordered
  • Storage: volumeClaimTemplates creates a PVC per Pod

The trade-off is operational rigidity. StatefulSets are slower to scale (one Pod at a time, sequentially), slower to roll out updates, and more complex to debug. Use them when identity matters — and stick with Deployments otherwise.

⏸ Pause and check

What's the difference between a Pod and a Deployment?

Answer: b. The Pod is what runs your container. The Deployment is the loop that creates Pods, watches them, and replaces failed ones — automatically and forever.

Section 2 · Before & After

Manual Pod management vs declared workloads

Before · raw Pods

You write five YAML files for five Pods. You apply them. One crashes — you re-apply. Demand doubles — you write five more YAML files. A node fails — you SSH in, find which Pods were on that node, recreate them on healthy nodes by hand.

Updates are surgery. To roll out a new version: stop one Pod, apply the new spec, wait, stop the next one, apply, wait. If something breaks halfway, you have a mix of old and new Pods and no clean rollback.

After · workload controllers

You write one Deployment YAML with replicas: 5. The controller creates the five Pods. A Pod crashes — replaced in seconds. A node fails — the missing Pods are rescheduled. Demand doubles — change one number, the controller scales.

Updates are kubectl set image deployment/my-app web=my-app:v2. Rolling update happens automatically — new Pods come up healthy before old ones go down. Rollback is kubectl rollout undo. The cluster never has more than 25% unavailable during the transition.

What didn't change: the underlying desire. You still want N copies of your app running. The controller turns that desire into a never-ending loop instead of a one-time imperative command.

Section 3 · Analogy

The K-Town Dispatch Office

Imagine the city's Dispatch Office — a corner building near city hall, full of clipboards, ticket-takers, and a wall of work orders. Businesses send standing orders to the dispatcher: "I need five baristas at all times in the morning shift," "I need a permanent night-watchman at the Bank Vault Quarter," "We need a one-time crew to repaint the harbour fence next Tuesday."

The dispatcher reads each order, sends workers to fill the slots, and watches. If a barista calls in sick, the dispatcher sends a replacement — same role, different person. If a watchman quits, the dispatcher posts the position again. If a one-time crew finishes the harbour fence, that work order closes. New shift requirements arrive, the dispatcher adjusts headcounts.

Different businesses have different needs, so the Dispatch Office issues five kinds of standing work order. Some bring rotating shift workers (any qualified person fills the slot — like baristas); others bring assigned-seat employees (Anna at desk 0, Brian at desk 1 — like the bank's tellers); others bring the one-per-building watchman; others bring one-time crews; others bring scheduled maintenance teams. Same dispatcher, same loop, five shapes of work.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The Dispatch OfficeA workload controller
A standing work orderA workload spec in YAML
"Rotating shift" job postingA Deployment (interchangeable Pods)
"Assigned seat" employeesA StatefulSet (Pods with stable identity)
"One-per-building" watchmanA DaemonSet (one Pod per node)
One-time work orderA Job
Scheduled maintenance roundA CronJob
Headcount on each postingThe replicas field (desired count)
Sending a replacement when someone leavesThe controller creating a new Pod

⚠️ The analogy stops here: real workforce dispatch keeps the same people across shifts. Kubernetes Deployments routinely terminate and replace Pods — same role, different instances. The "shift" is permanent; the workers are not.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

Imagine you have toy soldiers and a friend who watches them for you. You tell your friend, "I want five soldiers standing on the rug at all times." If a soldier falls over, your friend stands a new one up. If you say, "I want eight now," your friend gets three more. If you say, "Switch them all to blue uniforms," your friend swaps them in one at a time. Your friend never stops watching. That's a workload controller.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

A Pod runs your app, but a single Pod is fragile — if it crashes or its node dies, nothing brings it back. A workload controller is a small program (one of five flavours) that keeps a declared number of Pods running, reconciling reality against your spec forever. Most apps use a Deployment: declare replicas: 5 and the controller maintains exactly five healthy copies. Apps that need stable identity (databases, queues) use a StatefulSet. Workloads that should run on every node (logging, monitoring) use a DaemonSet. One-time tasks use Jobs; scheduled tasks use CronJobs. Same loop, five shapes.

⏸ Pause and check

Your team is running PostgreSQL with three replicas. The replicas need to find each other by stable hostname for replication. Should they use a Deployment or a StatefulSet?

Answer: b. StatefulSets give each Pod a stable name and a per-Pod persistent volume that survives restarts. Deployments treat Pods as interchangeable, which breaks replication.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where each controller shows up

A SaaS startup running 30 stateless services

Three engineers, weekly releases. Every service is a Deployment with 3-10 replicas. Releases are kubectl set image; the cluster rolls 25% at a time, never serving more than 25% old. Rollbacks happen via kubectl rollout undo; they happen monthly and are non-events. "We never write kubectl scale by hand — we change the YAML and let it reconcile."

A regional bank running Cassandra across three AZs

Cassandra needs each node to know its peers by stable hostname. The team uses a StatefulSet with 6 replicas spread across three availability zones (cassandra-0 through cassandra-5). Each Pod's PVC is recreated on the same persistent volume during restarts, so the Cassandra cluster's state survives Pod churn. Bootstrap took planning; daily ops are quiet.

A telecom running a metrics agent on every node

A Prometheus node-exporter scrapes per-node metrics. The team uses a DaemonSet — one agent per node, scheduled automatically when new nodes come up. Decommissioning a node automatically removes the agent. Adding a 50th node took zero ops effort; the DaemonSet noticed and deployed.

A media company encoding videos overnight

Every night at 1 AM, a CronJob fires that creates a Job for each video uploaded the previous day. Each Job runs a Pod with FFmpeg, transcodes the video into multiple bitrates, uploads to S3, and exits. Failed transcodes retry up to three times (the Job's backoffLimit). The team wakes up to a queue of completed transcodes, with failures already escalated.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch a Deployment reconcile — and roll out an update

Three modes: steady-state with a crash recovery, a scale-out, and a rolling update. The Dispatch Office (the controller) keeps the right number and version of workers at the cluster.

Watching: 3 Pods running, controller at idle. Wait for the crash, then see the replacement appear.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: Pods and Deployments are the same thing.
Truth: A Pod is a single running instance. A Deployment is a controller that maintains a declared number of identical Pods, replacing failed ones automatically and rolling out updates safely.
Myth: I can use a Deployment for my database.
Truth: Use a StatefulSet for stateful workloads (databases, queues, file servers). Deployments treat Pods as interchangeable; databases need stable identity, predictable startup order, and per-Pod persistent storage.
Myth: Rolling updates restart all Pods at once.
Truth: Rolling updates create new Pods one batch at a time and only retire old Pods after the new ones report ready. The cluster stays available throughout, with at most maxUnavailable (default 25%) gone at any moment.
What is a workload controller?
tap to flip
A small program watching forever that keeps a declared number of Pods running. You write the spec; the controller compares it against actual cluster state and creates or deletes Pods to close the gap.
Five workload controllers — what each is for?
tap to flip
Deployment (stateless apps, rolling updates). StatefulSet (stable identity, ordered, per-Pod PVC). DaemonSet (one Pod per node). Job (run-once). CronJob (scheduled Job).
Pod vs Deployment?
tap to flip
Pod = a single running instance. Deployment = controller maintaining N identical Pods. You almost never create raw Pods directly in production.
Deployment vs ReplicaSet?
tap to flip
ReplicaSet keeps N Pods alive (one version). Deployment manages ReplicaSets, enabling rolling updates and rollback by transitioning between ReplicaSets.
When use StatefulSet?
tap to flip
When Pods need stable identity (db-0, db-1, db-2), ordered startup/shutdown, or per-Pod persistent storage that survives restarts. Examples: Postgres, Cassandra, Kafka, Elasticsearch.
What is a DaemonSet?
tap to flip
A controller that runs exactly one Pod per node (or per matching node). Use for log collectors, monitoring agents, network plugins. Scales automatically as you add or remove nodes.
Job vs CronJob?
tap to flip
Job = run a Pod to completion once (with retries on failure). CronJob = create a fresh Job on a cron schedule (0 2 * * * = daily at 2 AM).
Rolling-update knobs?
tap to flip
maxSurge = max extra Pods above desired during update (default 25%). maxUnavailable = max Pods that can be down (default 25%). Together they bound how aggressive the rollout is.
How do I roll back?
tap to flip
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app reverts to the previous ReplicaSet (which is kept at zero replicas after the rollout). Single command. kubectl rollout history shows past revisions.

A team wants to roll out a new image version with zero downtime. They edit the Deployment's image tag and apply. What does Kubernetes do?

answerThe Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet at zero replicas, scales it up by maxSurge (default 25%) — those new Pods pull v2 and report ready. Once ready, the Deployment scales the old ReplicaSet down by maxUnavailable (default 25%). It alternates until the new ReplicaSet has all replicas and the old has zero. If a new Pod fails its readiness probe, the rollout pauses; the old Pods keep serving. kubectl rollout status shows progress; kubectl rollout undo reverts.

You need to run a Postgres backup every night at 1 AM. Which workload controller, and what does the spec look like at a high level?

answerA CronJob. Spec carries the cron schedule (0 1 * * *) and a Pod template that runs pg_dump, uploads to S3, and exits. The CronJob controller fires at each scheduled tick by creating a Job, which creates a Pod, which runs the backup script and terminates. Set backoffLimit for retries on failure, concurrencyPolicy: Forbid if a previous run is still going, and successfulJobsHistoryLimit to keep recent runs visible.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

Your team uses a Deployment for their three Postgres replicas. During a rolling update at 2 PM, all three Pods get terminated and replaced with fresh ones — in random order, with fresh IPs. Click to see what happens. ▼

2 PM and 30 seconds laterReplication breaks. The "new primary" doesn't know who its secondaries are; the "new secondaries" can't find the old primary's IP. Reads and writes are inconsistent. Half the application dashboard shows yesterday's data. Fix: switch to a StatefulSet. Each Pod gets a stable hostname (db-0, db-1, db-2), startup is ordered (db-0 comes up first as primary), each Pod's persistent volume is re-attached on restart, and rolling updates respect the order. Deployments are for stateless work; StatefulSets are for everything that has "who am I" as part of its job.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
Workload controller
A controller that maintains a declared number of Pods of a particular shape. Five built-in flavours: Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob.
Deployment
Manages a set of identical, interchangeable Pods. Default for stateless apps. Supports rolling updates and rollback.
ReplicaSet
Lower-level controller that keeps N Pods of a single template alive. Deployments manage ReplicaSets; you rarely create one directly.
StatefulSet
Workload controller giving each Pod a stable identity (name-N), per-Pod PVC, and ordered startup / shutdown.
DaemonSet
Workload controller that runs one Pod on every node (or every matching node). Scales automatically as nodes come and go.
Job
Workload controller that runs Pods to completion. Used for batch tasks. Retries failed Pods up to backoffLimit.
CronJob
Creates a fresh Job on a cron schedule. Used for scheduled batch tasks (backups, reports, cache warms).
Rolling update
Deployment's default update strategy: bring up new Pods in batches, retire old Pods only after new ones are ready.
maxSurge / maxUnavailable
Knobs on the rolling-update strategy. Surge = max extra Pods above desired during the update. Unavailable = max Pods that can be down. Both default to 25%.
HorizontalPodAutoscaler
A separate controller that auto-scales a Deployment's replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. Scales up under load, down at idle.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Pods come and go. Workload controllers keep your apps running at the right shape — five flavours: Deployment for stateless apps, StatefulSet for apps with identity, DaemonSet for one-per-node, Job for run-once, CronJob for run-on-schedule.

✓ Lesson 16 complete

Pods are the unit of work. Workload controllers keep them at the right shape, forever. Five flavours match the five common shapes of work, all running the same reconciliation loop.

Next — Lesson 17: Services & Networking. Pods restart and get new IPs. So how do other Pods reach them? How does the outside world reach them? Four flavours of Service, plus Ingress, plus NetworkPolicy. The K-Town Switchboard.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 16 of 45 · grounded in kubernetes.io
Lesson 17· Services & Networking · ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, Ingress, NetworkPolicy

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: K-Town Switchboard.

📍 Switchboard · lesson 17 of 45Lesson 17 of 45, K-Town Switchboard.

Module 4 · Lesson 17 · the course finale

Services & Networking · ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, Ingress, NetworkPolicy

Pods come and go; their IPs come with them. Services give your Pods a stable name. Ingress lets the outside world reach them. NetworkPolicy controls who can talk to whom.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

Your front-end app's database connection string was hardcoded to a Pod IP. 10.0.5.7. It worked yesterday. Tonight, the database Pod restarts (a normal event — node maintenance). It comes back up with a fresh IP, 10.0.6.2. Your front-end keeps trying 10.0.5.7. Every API call returns 500. By morning the support queue is at 200 and you're explaining to your boss what happened. This lesson is about why Pods don't have stable IPs — and the four flavours of Service that do.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Pods come and go; their IPs come with them. Services give your Pods a stable name. Four flavours of Service plus Ingress for HTTP plus NetworkPolicy for who-can-talk-to-whom.

Section 1 · Concept

Why Pods don't have stable IPs (and what does)

Each Pod gets its own IP address from the cluster's internal network. That IP works — Pods can reach each other directly by IP. The problem is that Pod IPs are ephemeral. When a Pod restarts, dies, or gets rescheduled to another node, it gets a fresh IP. There's no way to predict in advance what IP the new Pod will have. Hardcoding a Pod IP is hardcoding tomorrow's outage.

A Service is a Kubernetes object that gives a group of Pods a stable name and a stable network identity. You declare a Service that selects Pods by labels (app: my-api). The Service tracks which Pods match — when Pods come and go, the Service's backend list updates automatically. Anything inside the cluster reaches the Service by its DNS name (my-api.default.svc.cluster.local) and the kubelet's networking layer routes the request to one of the matching Pods.

Four Service types exist for four scopes of access:

Section 1.5 · The four Service types

Each type is a different scope of "reachable from"

The default is ClusterIP. The other three add increasingly external reach. Pick the smallest scope that fits.

📞

ClusterIP

internal directory

A virtual IP reachable only from inside the cluster. Other Pods reach the Service by DNS name. The default and most common type — every internal microservice talks to others through ClusterIPs.

When: Pod-to-Pod traffic inside the cluster.

📱

NodePort

phone booth on every block

Exposes the Service on a high port (30000–32767) on every node. External traffic to any-node-IP:30080 reaches the Service. Useful for development, custom load balancers, or specific traffic-shaping needs.

When: on-prem clusters without a cloud LB; bare-metal setups.

📡

LoadBalancer

dispatch operator

On a cloud cluster, asks the cloud-controller-manager to provision an external load balancer (an ELB on AWS, an NLB on GCP, an Azure LB) with a public IP. Traffic to that IP routes to all backing Pods.

When: exposing a non-HTTP service on a managed cloud cluster.

🪧

ExternalName

phone-book alias

Doesn't have any Pods or selectors. Just a CNAME-style DNS alias mapping the Service name to an external DNS name (db.example.com). Lets in-cluster code reach external services through the same naming convention.

When: integrating with managed databases, third-party APIs, or migrating workloads in.

Section 1.7 · Ingress in detail

HTTP / HTTPS routing: when LoadBalancer per service is too much

If every public-facing service had its own LoadBalancer, a 30-microservice product would burn 30 cloud LBs (and 30× the bill). Ingress solves this: one LoadBalancer in front of an Ingress controller, and an Ingress resource describes the routing rules. api.example.com/users goes to the user-service; api.example.com/orders goes to the order-service; app.example.com goes to the front-end.

Ingress is layer 7 — it speaks HTTP / HTTPS. It can do host-based routing (app.example.com vs api.example.com), path-based routing (/users vs /orders), TLS termination (Ingress holds the cert; backend services see plain HTTP), redirects, rewrites, basic auth, and rate-limiting if the controller supports it.

The Ingress resource

  • A YAML object describing routing rules
  • Doesn't run any traffic itself — it's just configuration
  • Specifies host, path, backing Service, TLS
  • Lives in the namespace that owns the routes

The Ingress controller

  • The actual proxy that processes traffic
  • Watches Ingress objects and configures itself
  • Examples: nginx-ingress, Traefik, HAProxy, Contour
  • Cluster has at least one (sometimes several, by class)

Gateway API is the next-generation replacement for Ingress — same job, more expressive, with proper role-separated APIs (cluster operators define gateways; app teams define routes). New clusters often ship Gateway API alongside Ingress; both work.

Section 1.9 · NetworkPolicy in detail

Who can talk to whom — by default, everyone

By default in Kubernetes, every Pod can reach every other Pod. Your front-end can talk to the database. Your database can talk to the public internet. A compromised Pod has full network reach. That's the default; it's not necessarily what you want.

NetworkPolicy is the firewall. You write rules saying "Pods labelled tier: frontend can talk to Pods labelled tier: backend on port 8080, and nothing else." Once a Pod is selected by any NetworkPolicy, it becomes default-deny — only the rules you've written allow traffic. Pods not selected by any policy stay default-allow.

Default-deny ingress

Empty ingress: [] rule selecting all Pods. After applying: nothing inbound reaches any Pod unless you write an explicit allow rule. Standard hardening pattern.

Allow frontend → backend

Selector: tier: backend. Rule: from: { podSelector: { tier: frontend } }, ports: [{port: 8080}]. Frontend Pods can reach backend on port 8080; nothing else can.

Default-deny egress

Empty egress: [] rule selecting compromise-prone Pods. They can't reach the internet, the API server, or other namespaces unless explicitly allowed. Containment by default.

Allow DNS

Almost every Pod needs DNS. Allow egress to kube-system namespace's DNS Pods on UDP 53. Without this, name resolution breaks.

[ deep dive — skip if new ]NetworkPolicy enforcement is done by the CNI plugin — Calico, Cilium, Weave Net, etc. Vanilla kube-proxy doesn't enforce NetworkPolicy on its own; you need a CNI that supports it. Check your cluster's CNI before assuming policies work — applying a NetworkPolicy on a CNI that ignores them silently leaves you wide open.

⏸ Pause and check

A Pod restarts. Its IP changes. What still works to find it?

Answer: b. Services use label selectors to find their backing Pods, then resolve a stable DNS name to whatever Pods currently match. Pod restart → new IP → Service notices and updates its backend list automatically.

Section 2 · Before & After

Raw Pod IPs vs Service abstraction

Before · raw Pod IPs

You connect your front-end to the database by IP. Every Pod restart breaks something. You write scripts to scrape kubectl get pods -o wide for current IPs. You hardcode IPs in config files; you update those config files manually after every deploy.

Cross-cluster portability is impossible — your IPs are valid on this cluster only. You can't load-balance across multiple Pod replicas because there's no abstraction over them. Every change is brittle.

After · Services + DNS

You declare apiVersion: v1, kind: Service with a label selector and a port. Inside the cluster, anything reaches the Service via DNS: postgres.default.svc.cluster.local (or just postgres in the same namespace). The Service tracks which Pods match and load-balances across them.

Pod restarts — invisible. Pods scale up — Service notices and includes them. Cluster moves to a new namespace or environment — names stay the same, IPs change underneath. Add an Ingress and the outside world reaches your services through one cloud LB instead of one per service.

What didn't change: the Pods themselves. They still run on ephemeral IPs. The Service is a layer of indirection between callers and Pods — and that indirection is exactly what makes the system survive Pod churn.

Section 3 · Analogy

The K-Town Switchboard

K-Town's office buildings used to have direct phone lines — call the bakery's specific instrument and you got the bakery. The problem: instruments moved. The bakery rearranged its kitchen, the hallways were renumbered, the line that used to be 5567 became 5824. Anyone with the old number got a wrong number; mail got lost.

So the city built the Switchboard — a central exchange. Now you don't dial the bakery's instrument. You ask for "Bakery District" and the operator patches your call through to whatever instrument the bakery is currently using. The bakery moves its phones around all it likes; the operator's directory is updated continuously, and your call still lands.

Different kinds of calls need different switchboard treatments. Calls between K-Town businesses go through the internal directory (anyone can dial Bakery and reach it). Calls from out-of-town that arrive on a public phone booth on each block go through NodePort. Calls that need a single famous public number routed to the right department use the dispatch operator (LoadBalancer). And HTTP-shaped calls — traffic that arrives at the city's main turnstile — get routed by Ingress based on which website the caller asked for. Plus the city's traffic rules — NetworkPolicy — decide who's allowed to call whom in the first place.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The SwitchboardThe Service abstraction
The instrument's number on a deskA Pod IP (ephemeral)
The name in the directoryThe Service DNS name
An internal phone directoryA ClusterIP Service
A public phone booth on each blockA NodePort Service
The dispatch operator routing calls to the next free boothA LoadBalancer Service
The main-entrance turnstile reading destination labelsAn Ingress (or Gateway API)
A phone-book alias to an out-of-town numberAn ExternalName Service
The city's traffic rules — who can call whomNetworkPolicy

⚠️ The analogy stops here: real telephone exchanges have one switchboard at the centre. Kubernetes runs many networking components in parallel — the CNI plugin, kube-proxy on every node, the cluster DNS, the cloud LB. The "switchboard" is distributed.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

Imagine your friends keep moving to new houses, but you still want to call them. Instead of remembering everyone's new address every time, you just call the operator and say "I want to talk to Sam!" The operator knows where Sam lives right now and connects you. That's a Kubernetes Service. The Pods (your friends) move around. The Service (the operator) always knows where to find them.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

Pods get IPs that change every restart, so you can't talk to a Pod by IP. A Service gives a group of Pods (selected by labels) a stable name and a stable virtual IP. Other Pods reach the Service by DNS — my-api.default.svc.cluster.local — and the cluster's networking layer routes the request to one of the matching Pods. Four Service types: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (exposed on each node), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), ExternalName (DNS alias). For HTTP traffic, an Ingress routes by hostname and path, terminating TLS — one cloud LB shared across many services. NetworkPolicy firewalls Pod-to-Pod traffic.

⏸ Pause and check

You want to expose a web app to the internet on a managed cluster (EKS, GKE, AKS). Which Service type is the natural fit?

Answer: c. LoadBalancer triggers the cloud-controller-manager to provision a managed LB. For multiple HTTP services, share one LB by putting an Ingress controller in front of it.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where each piece shows up

A SaaS company with 40 microservices

Public traffic enters one nginx Ingress controller (one cloud LB). The Ingress resource defines routes: api.acme.com/users → user-service, api.acme.com/billing → billing-service, app.acme.com → frontend. TLS terminated at the Ingress; backends see plain HTTP. One cert, one LB, 40 services routed by URL. Saves ~$3,000/month vs LoadBalancer-per-service.

A trading firm running internal-only services

Every internal service is ClusterIP. NetworkPolicy enforces default-deny on every namespace. Specific allow rules let trading apps reach Postgres + Redis on specific ports; everything else is blocked, including the public internet. A compromised app can't even DNS-lookup an external host.

A logging platform on a DaemonSet

Fluent Bit runs as a DaemonSet — one Pod per node. App Pods write logs to localhost:24224; the local Fluent Bit batches and ships them to a central log store. No Service needed for that internal communication — the agent is on the same node, reachable via hostNetwork. Cuts cross-node traffic for logs to zero.

A multi-tenant cluster with namespace isolation

Each tenant gets a namespace. NetworkPolicy uses namespace selectors to enforce: tenant Pods can talk inside their own namespace + to kube-system (DNS) + to monitoring (Prometheus scraping) — and that's it. Cross-tenant traffic is blocked at the network layer. RBAC controls who can create resources; NetworkPolicy controls how the resources communicate.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch a request find its Pod — three modes

Three modes show how a request travels from caller to a Pod through different Service types. The Pods are the same in all three; only the routing changes.

Watching: a Pod calls http://api. The Service resolves the name to its ClusterIP, picks a backing Pod, and forwards the request.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: Services have IPs, so the IP is the right thing to use in app config.
Truth: Use the Service name (DNS), not the Service IP. The Service IP is stable but coupling to it ties you to one cluster's CIDR. Names work everywhere.
Myth: A LoadBalancer Service is the only way to expose HTTP traffic.
Truth: For HTTP, an Ingress controller (or Gateway API) is more flexible — host and path routing, TLS termination, one cloud LB shared across many services. LoadBalancer is for non-HTTP or single-service setups.
Myth: NetworkPolicy is on by default; my Pods are isolated.
Truth: By default every Pod can talk to every other Pod in the cluster. NetworkPolicy is opt-in — you write rules to restrict, otherwise everything is open. Default-deny is a posture you have to configure.
Why don't Pods have stable IPs?
tap to flip
Pod IPs are assigned from the cluster network at scheduling time. When a Pod restarts, dies, or is rescheduled, it gets a fresh IP. Hardcoding a Pod IP is hardcoding tomorrow's outage.
Four Service types?
tap to flip
ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (each node's high port), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), ExternalName (DNS alias to external host).
When use ClusterIP?
tap to flip
For Pod-to-Pod traffic inside the cluster. The default. The most common type — every internal microservice gets one.
When use LoadBalancer?
tap to flip
On a cloud cluster, exposing a non-HTTP service (or a single HTTP service) to the public internet. The cloud-controller-manager provisions an external LB.
Ingress vs Service?
tap to flip
A Service exposes a group of Pods at L4 (TCP/UDP). An Ingress is an L7 (HTTP/HTTPS) router in front of Services — host-based and path-based routing, TLS termination, shared LB.
Headless Service?
tap to flip
A Service with clusterIP: None. DNS returns the IPs of all backing Pods directly (multiple A records) instead of a single virtual IP. Used for StatefulSets where callers need each Pod's identity.
NetworkPolicy default behaviour?
tap to flip
